Moses stands before a generation that has only known wilderness—forty years of God's provision, God's discipline, and God's constant presence. Now they face abundance. The land ahead flows with water and overflows with crops, a place so fertile that their hunger will vanish and their wealth will multiply. This is the moment of greatest spiritual danger, not because God's gifts are evil, but because satisfied people forget who satisfied them. Moses is not warning against prosperity itself; he is warning against the amnesia that prosperity breeds.

The chapter traces a spiritual trajectory that remains achingly familiar. Israel experienced God's humbling and testing in the desert—manna instead of bread, water from rock instead of wells, clothes that didn't wear out as a sign of divine care. These were not accidents or lucky breaks but deliberate acts of a Father disciplining His children so they would understand that life flows from God's word, not from anything they could provide for themselves. But Moses knows what will happen next. When their bellies are full and their houses are fine and their herds are vast, they will take credit for it. They will whisper to themselves, "The power and strength of my hands have made this wealth for me." They will look at what they built and forget who gave them the ability to build anything at all.

What makes this chapter convicting is that Moses is not describing a distant possibility—he is describing a certainty. "If you ever forget the LORD your God and go after other gods to worship and bow down to them, I testify against you today that you will surely perish" (verse 19). This is not a warning about potential consequences; this is a witnessed verdict delivered in advance. The nations that came before Israel learned nothing from their discipline and were destroyed. Israel will face the same choice and the same stakes. The remedy Moses offers is equally clear: remember. Bless the LORD when you eat and are satisfied. Keep His commandments. Walk in His ways. Fear Him. The discipline of remembrance is not an optional spiritual practice—it is the difference between life and death.

The deepest challenge of this chapter lies in recognizing that prosperity genuinely obscures God from the human heart in ways that deprivation does not. When you are hungry and God provides manna, you know it is God. When you are thirsty and water comes from rock, you cannot credit yourself. But when you have worked hard, when you have managed well, when your fields yield and your businesses flourish, the temptation to believe in your own power becomes almost irresistible. This is why verse 18 is so essential: God gives you the power to gain wealth precisely so you will remember the covenant, so you will stay tethered to Him, so you will not perish like the nations before you. The test of abundance is harder than the test of wilderness, and it requires constant, deliberate remembrance. What are you forgetting now that you have obtained what you once longed for?
